Description: Algorithmic Complexity
Refactor_Advice: Analyze the algorithm's time complexity. Replace inefficient algorithms (e.g., nested loops) with optimized data structures or algorithms.

Description: Missing Default Case in Switch Statement
Refactor_Advice: Always provide a default case in switch constructs to explicitly handle unexpected values or log anomalies.

Description: Use of Incorrect Operator
Refactor_Advice: Review conditional and mathematical expressions to ensure the correct operator is used (e.g., using '==' instead of '=').

Description: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
Refactor_Advice: Insert 'break' statements at the end of each switch case block to prevent unintended fall-through execution.

Description: Dead code
Refactor_Advice: Identify and remove unreachable code segments, unused functions, or obsolete conditional branches to reduce maintenance overhead.

Description: Expression is Always False
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the logical condition. Remove the dead conditional block or correct the logic so it can evaluate dynamically.

Description: Expression is Always True
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the logical condition. Remove the redundant check and execute the code block directly, or fix the logic.

Description: Operator Precedence Logic Error
Refactor_Advice: Use explicit parentheses in complex expressions to force the intended order of operations and clarify logic.

Description: Use of Redundant Code (Copy-Paste)
Refactor_Advice: Extract duplicate or redundant code patterns shared across multiple locations into a single, reusable function or method.

Description: Parent Class with a Virtual Destructor and a Child Class without a Virtual Destructor
Refactor_Advice: Declare the destructor as virtual in the child class to ensure proper cleanup of derived object resources.

Description: Modules with Circular Dependencies
Refactor_Advice: Break circular dependencies by extracting common functionality into a shared interface or utilizing Dependency Injection.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Large Number of Outward Calls (Excessive Coupling or Fan-out)
Refactor_Advice: Decouple classes by applying the Single Responsibility Principle and abstracting outward calls behind cohesive interfaces.

Description: Initialization with Hard-Coded Network Resource Configuration Data
Refactor_Advice: Move hard-coded network identifiers (like IP addresses or URLs) to external configuration files or environment variables.

Description: Excessive Use of Hard-Coded Literals in Initialization
Refactor_Advice: Replace hard-coded literal strings and 'magic numbers' with named constants, enums, or configuration parameters.

Description: Invocation of a Control Element at an Unnecessarily Deep Horizontal Layer (Layer-skipping Call)
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the architecture to enforce strict layering; route calls sequentially through adjacent layers to reduce tight coupling.

Description: Multiple Inheritance from Concrete Classes
Refactor_Advice: Replace multiple inheritance with object composition or inherit from multiple interfaces to avoid the diamond problem.

Description: Parent Class Element with References to Child Class
Refactor_Advice: Remove downward dependencies. If a parent needs child-specific behavior, use abstract methods or the Template Method pattern.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Signature Containing an Excessive Number of Parameters
Refactor_Advice: Encapsulate excessive parameters into a dedicated data transfer object (DTO) or configuration struct.

Description: Class with Excessively Deep Inheritance
Refactor_Advice: Favor composition over inheritance. Flatten the inheritance hierarchy to reduce coupling and improve navigability.

Description: Unconditional Control Flow Transfer outside of Switch Block
Refactor_Advice: Eliminate 'goto' statements. Refactor logic using standard structured control flow mechanisms like if/else blocks or loops.

Description: Parent Class without Virtual Destructor Method
Refactor_Advice: Declare the destructor in the base class as virtual to guarantee correct polymorphic destruction of derived instances.

Description: Source Code File with Excessive Number of Lines of Code
Refactor_Advice: Refactor massive files by splitting them into smaller, highly cohesive modules based on specific domain logic.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Excessive File or Data Access Operations
Refactor_Advice: Batch database queries or delegate data access responsibilities to a dedicated Data Access Object (DAO) layer.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Excessive Volume of Commented-out Code
Refactor_Advice: Delete commented-out code blocks completely and rely on version control systems for historical code tracking.

Description: Class with Excessive Number of Child Classes
Refactor_Advice: Review the inheritance tree and consider extracting common behaviors into interfaces or utilizing the Strategy pattern.

Description: Class with Virtual Method without a Virtual Destructor
Refactor_Advice: Add a virtual destructor to any polymorphic class to prevent resource leaks when instances are deleted.

Description: Method Containing Access of a Member Element from Another Class
Refactor_Advice: Enforce encapsulation by making the member private and accessing it only via public getter/setter methods.

Description: Loop Condition Value Update within the Loop
Refactor_Advice: Ensure loop control variables are updated exclusively in the loop signature to prevent unpredictable control flow logic.

Description: Excessive McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity
Refactor_Advice: Reduce branching by extracting complex nested if/else logic into smaller helper functions or using polymorphism.

Description: Modules with Circular Dependencies
Refactor_Advice: Break circular dependencies by extracting common functionality into a shared interface or utilizing Dependency Injection (DI).

Description: Invokable Control Element with Large Number of Outward Calls (Excessive Coupling)
Refactor_Advice: Decouple classes by applying the Single Responsibility Principle and abstracting outward calls behind cohesive interfaces or Facades.

Description: Initialization with Hard-Coded Network Resource Configuration Data
Refactor_Advice: Move hard-coded IP addresses or URLs to external configuration files, environment variables, or a centralized secret manager.

Description: Excessive Use of Hard-Coded Literals in Initialization
Refactor_Advice: Replace 'magic numbers' and hard-coded literal strings with named constants, enums, or configuration parameters.

Description: Invocation of a Control Element at an Unnecessarily Deep Horizontal Layer (Layer-skipping Call)
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the architecture to enforce strict layering; route calls sequentially through adjacent layers or utilize an API gateway.

Description: Multiple Inheritance from Concrete Classes
Refactor_Advice: Replace multiple inheritance with object composition or inherit from multiple interfaces/traits to avoid the diamond problem.

Description: Parent Class Element with References to Child Class
Refactor_Advice: Remove downward dependencies. If a parent needs child-specific behavior, use abstract methods, generics, or the Template Method pattern.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Signature Containing an Excessive Number of Parameters
Refactor_Advice: Encapsulate excessive parameters into a dedicated data transfer object (DTO), configuration struct, or use the Builder pattern.

Description: Class with Excessively Deep Inheritance
Refactor_Advice: Favor composition over inheritance. Flatten the inheritance hierarchy to reduce tight coupling and improve code navigability.

Description: Unconditional Control Flow Transfer outside of Switch Block
Refactor_Advice: Eliminate 'goto' statements. Refactor the logic using standard structured control flow mechanisms like if/else blocks or loops.

Description: Parent Class without Virtual Destructor Method
Refactor_Advice: Declare the destructor in the parent class as virtual to guarantee proper polymorphic resource cleanup in derived classes.

Description: Source Code File with Excessive Number of Lines of Code
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the 'God class' or massive file by splitting it into smaller, highly cohesive modules based on specific domain logic.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Excessive File or Data Access Operations
Refactor_Advice: Batch database queries or delegate data access responsibilities to a dedicated Data Access Object (DAO) layer.

Description: Invokable Control Element with Excessive Volume of Commented-out Code
Refactor_Advice: Delete commented-out code blocks completely and rely on your version control system (e.g., Git) for historical code tracking.

Description: Class with Excessive Number of Child Classes
Refactor_Advice: Review the inheritance tree. Consider extracting common behaviors into interfaces or utilizing the Strategy design pattern.

Description: Class with Virtual Method without a Virtual Destructor
Refactor_Advice: Add a virtual destructor to the class to ensure safe deletion of derived class instances via base class pointers.

Description: Method Containing Access of a Member Element from Another Class
Refactor_Advice: Enforce encapsulation by making the member private and accessing it only via public getter/setter methods.

Description: Loop Condition Value Update within the Loop
Refactor_Advice: Ensure loop control variables are updated exclusively in the loop signature (e.g., the 'for' loop header) to prevent logic errors.

Description: Excessive McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity
Refactor_Advice: Reduce branching by extracting complex nested if/else logic into smaller helper functions or using polymorphism.

Description: Improper Resource Shutdown or Release
Refactor_Advice: Guarantee resource closure by utilizing try-with-resources statements or explicit 'finally' blocks.

Description: Improper Release of Memory Before Removing Last Reference ('Memory Leak')
Refactor_Advice: Utilize RAII/smart pointers or ensure explicit deallocation of memory objects before references go out of scope.

Description: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Release network connections, threads, or memory immediately after their functional use has completed.

Description: Missing Release of File Descriptor or Handle after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Always explicitly close file descriptors/handles in error-handling 'finally' blocks to prevent descriptor exhaustion.

Description: Improper Protection of Alternate Path
Refactor_Advice: Implement a unified authentication and authorization middleware to secure all potential access paths to the data.

Description: Static Member Data Element outside of a Singleton Class Element
Refactor_Advice: Remove the static modifier or safely refactor the class structure into a thread-safe Singleton pattern.

Description: Data Element Aggregating an Excessively Large Number of Non-Primitive Elements
Refactor_Advice: Break down the monolithic data structure into smaller, highly cohesive, and lazily-loaded components.

Description: Creation of Immutable Text Using String Concatenation
Refactor_Advice: Replace repeated '+' string concatenations within iterations with a mutable StringBuilder or StringBuffer.

Description: Excessive Data Query Operations in a Large Data Table
Refactor_Advice: Optimize the query by reducing nested sub-queries, minimizing JOINs, and utilizing database materialized views.

Description: Excessive Platform Resource Consumption within a Loop
Refactor_Advice: Extract resource-heavy operations (e.g., database connections, file I/O) out of the loop body.

Description: Data Access Operations Outside of Expected Data Manager Component
Refactor_Advice: Route all database or file access operations strictly through the designated Data Access Object (DAO) component.

Description: Excessive Number of Inefficient Server-Side Data Accesses
Refactor_Advice: Consolidate multiple individual data queries into a single batch request or utilize a database stored procedure.

Description: Excessive Execution of Sequential Searches of Data Resource
Refactor_Advice: Add appropriate primary or composite indices to the database table to eliminate full table sequential scans.

Description: Data Resource Access without Use of Connection Pooling
Refactor_Advice: Implement and configure a database connection pool to reuse established connections and reduce connection overhead.

Description: Non-SQL Invokable Control Element with Excessive Number of Data Resource Accesses
Refactor_Advice: Optimize client-side data retrieval by utilizing efficient server-side filtering or batching APIs.

Description: Large Data Table with Excessive Number of Indices
Refactor_Advice: Review and prune redundant or unused database indices to reduce write-operation overhead and storage footprint.

Description: Use of Object without Invoking Destructor Method
Refactor_Advice: Ensure the object's destructor or finalize method is explicitly invoked, or bind the object to a managed scope.

Description: Excessive Index Range Scan for a Data Resource
Refactor_Advice: Refine query predicates to narrow the range scan scope, or optimize the indexing strategy to support point lookups.

Description: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
Refactor_Advice: Enforce strict boundary checking before any memory buffer access, or utilize memory-safe language abstractions like dynamically sized collections.

Description: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
Refactor_Advice: Verify the size of the input buffer does not exceed the output buffer before copying, or use safe bounded copy functions (e.g., strncpy).

Description: Write-what-where condition
Refactor_Advice: Validate all pointer arithmetic and memory offsets to ensure they strictly remain within authorized memory segments.

Description: Out-of-bounds read
Refactor_Advice: Validate array indices and pointer offsets against allocated buffer limits before executing any read operations.

Description: Improper Handling of Length Parameter Inconsistency
Refactor_Advice: Validate that explicit length fields in parsed messages or structures strictly match the actual payload size before processing.

Description: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer
Refactor_Advice: Ensure pointer decrements and negative indices are boundary-checked to prevent reading or writing before the buffer's base address.

Description: Out-of-bounds Write
Refactor_Advice: Check bounds before writing to arrays or buffers, ensuring index variables and pointer arithmetic do not exceed allocated limits.

Description: Access of Memory Location After End of Buffer
Refactor_Advice: Prevent pointer increments from passing the buffer's tail end by enforcing strict upper-bound limits during iteration.

Description: Buffer Access with Incorrect Length Value
Refactor_Advice: Double-check length calculations passed to sequential read/write operations (like memcpy) to ensure they do not exceed actual buffer bounds.

Description: Untrusted Pointer Dereference
Refactor_Advice: Never directly convert untrusted input into pointers; validate and sanitize any external data used to reference memory locations.

Description: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset
Refactor_Advice: Validate that offset calculations derived from untrusted sources or complex arithmetic fall safely within the target structured data boundaries.

Description: Access of Uninitialized Pointer
Refactor_Advice: Initialize all pointers to NULL or a valid memory address immediately upon declaration.

Description: Expired Pointer Dereference
Refactor_Advice: Set pointers to NULL immediately after freeing the memory they point to, preventing dangling pointer usage.

Description: Improper Null Termination
Refactor_Advice: Ensure strings and character arrays are explicitly null-terminated, or replace raw character arrays with standard string objects.

Description: Unchecked Return Value
Refactor_Advice: Explicitly verify the return values of all method calls, particularly I/O and system APIs, and implement appropriate error-handling logic.

Description: Detection of Error Condition Without Action
Refactor_Advice: Populate empty catch blocks or error branches with meaningful handling logic, such as structured logging or state recovery.

Description: Unexpected Status Code or Return Value
Refactor_Advice: Implement comprehensive checks for all possible return status codes to handle unexpected states gracefully.

Description: Improper Resource Shutdown or Release
Refactor_Advice: Guarantee resource closure by utilizing try-with-resources statements or explicit 'finally' blocks.

Description: Improper Release of Memory Before Removing Last Reference ('Memory Leak')
Refactor_Advice: Utilize RAII/smart pointers or ensure explicit deallocation of memory objects before references go out of scope.

Description: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Release network connections, threads, or memory explicitly immediately after their functional use has completed.

Description: Missing Release of File Descriptor or Handle after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Always explicitly close file descriptors or handles in error-handling 'finally' blocks to prevent descriptor exhaustion.

Description: Improper Protection of Alternate Path
Refactor_Advice: Implement a unified authentication and authorization middleware to secure all potential access paths to restricted functionality.

Description: Incomplete Cleanup
Refactor_Advice: Ensure all temporary files, caches, and supporting resources are systematically deleted or scrubbed after their designated use.

Description: NULL Pointer Dereference
Refactor_Advice: Implement explicit pre-dereference null checks, or encapsulate nullable variables inside safe wrapper types.

Description: Use of Incorrect Operator
Refactor_Advice: Review logical and mathematical expressions to ensure correct operators are used (e.g., using '==' instead of '=').

Description: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
Refactor_Advice: Insert 'break' statements at the end of each switch case block to prevent unintended fall-through execution.

Description: Return of Stack Variable Address
Refactor_Advice: Avoid returning pointers or references to local stack variables; allocate memory dynamically on the heap or pass parameters by reference instead.

Description: Comparison of Object References Instead of Object Contents
Refactor_Advice: Use object-specific equality methods (e.g., .equals() in Java) instead of reference comparison operators (==) to compare object contents.

Description: Use of Wrong Operator in String Comparison
Refactor_Advice: Utilize dedicated string comparison methods rather than identity operators when evaluating string equivalence.

Description: Persistent Storable Data Element without Associated Comparison Control Element
Refactor_Advice: Ensure persistent classes fully implement necessary comparison and hashing methods (e.g., equals() and hashCode()) to maintain data integrity.

Description: Improper Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Synchronize access to shared mutable state using locks/mutexes or thread-safe concurrency primitives.

Description: Race Condition within a Thread
Refactor_Advice: Identify critical sections where shared resources are used concurrently and wrap them in mutual exclusion locks.

Description: Use of Singleton Pattern Without Synchronization in a Multithreaded Context
Refactor_Advice: Implement double-checked locking or utilize thread-safe static initialization for Singletons accessed across multiple threads.

Description: Unsynchronized Access to Shared Data in a Multithreaded Context
Refactor_Advice: Protect shared static variables or shared objects across threads by applying appropriate synchronization locks or using atomic variables.

Description: Improper Locking
Refactor_Advice: Ensure locks are acquired correctly and definitively released in a robust 'finally' block or via RAII.

Description: Multiple Locks of a Critical Resource
Refactor_Advice: Ensure proper lock tracking and avoid recursive locking unless using explicitly configured reentrant locks.

Description: Missing Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Identify concurrent access points to shared resources and apply appropriate synchronization mechanisms to ensure memory visibility.

Description: Incorrect Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Review synchronization blocks to ensure they lock on the correct shared object monitor rather than disparate or local instances.

Description: Invokable Control Element in Multi-Thread Context with non-Final Static Storable or Member Element
Refactor_Advice: Make static members final if they are read-only, or enforce synchronized access if they must be mutable in a multi-threaded context.

Description: Singleton Class Instance Creation without Proper Locking or Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Apply thread-safe instantiation techniques (e.g., eager initialization or double-checked locking) to the Singleton factory method.

Description: Improper Initialization
Refactor_Advice: Ensure complex resources are fully initialized and valid before their references are exposed to the rest of the application.

Description: Missing Initialization of a Variable
Refactor_Advice: Initialize all critical variables explicitly at the point of declaration or within the constructor.

Description: Use of uninitialized variable
Refactor_Advice: Assign a default or known safe value to variables before any read operations occur.

Description: Operation on a Resource after Expiration or Release
Refactor_Advice: Invalidate resource references immediately upon release and verify state flags before attempting subsequent operations.

Description: Double Free
Refactor_Advice: Nullify pointers immediately after freeing the associated memory, or adopt smart pointers to automate safe memory lifecycle management.

Description: Use After Free
Refactor_Advice: Eliminate dangling pointers by clearing references after memory release to prevent program crashes or unexpected behavior.

Description: Incorrect Conversion between Numeric Types
Refactor_Advice: Validate numeric ranges before performing type casting (e.g., long to int) to prevent data truncation or unexpected values.

Description: Unexpected Sign Extension
Refactor_Advice: Use unsigned data types when performing bitwise operations or upcasting to prevent negative numbers from causing unintended sign extensions.

Description: Signed to Unsigned Conversion Error
Refactor_Advice: Validate that a signed integer is positive before casting it to an unsigned type to prevent integer wrap-around errors.

Description: Unsigned to Signed Conversion Error
Refactor_Advice: Ensure the unsigned value does not exceed the maximum capacity of the target signed type before casting.

Description: Numeric Truncation Error
Refactor_Advice: Check bounds before downcasting primitive types (e.g., 64-bit to 32-bit) to prevent high-order bit data loss.

Description: Incorrect Calculation
Refactor_Advice: Audit arithmetic logic for critical decision-making or resource management code, adding comprehensive unit tests for mathematical correctness.

Description: Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size
Refactor_Advice: Double-check buffer allocation sizing logic, accounting for string null terminators and correct struct padding/sizing.

Description: Divide By Zero
Refactor_Advice: Add conditional guards to explicitly verify that the denominator is strictly non-zero before executing any division.

Description: Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions
Refactor_Advice: Anticipate edge cases and implement comprehensive try-catch mechanisms to handle conditions that disrupt normal flow.

Description: Uncaught Exception
Refactor_Advice: Ensure all thrown exceptions are caught and handled by an appropriate upstream catch block or global exception handler.

Description: Unchecked Error Condition
Refactor_Advice: Do not ignore error conditions or swallow exceptions silently; log them properly and handle the state transition.

Description: Missing Report of Error Condition
Refactor_Advice: Ensure functions encountering errors explicitly return informative status codes or throw structured exceptions to the caller.

Description: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast
Refactor_Advice: Use safe casting operators (e.g., dynamic_cast in C++, instanceof in Java) to verify object types before conversion.

Description: Reliance on Undefined, Unspecified, or Implementation-Defined Behavior
Refactor_Advice: Remove dependencies on compiler-specific behaviors and strictly adhere to standardized language and API contracts.

Description: Deadlock
Refactor_Advice: Refactor lock acquisition to follow a strict global hierarchy across all threads, or utilize timeout-based locking.

Description: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop')
Refactor_Advice: Review loop control variables to ensure they are properly updated within the loop body to reach the termination condition.

Description: Use of Uninitialized Resource
Refactor_Advice: Ensure resources and connections are completely initialized and verified before exposing them to business logic execution.

Description: Parent Class with a Virtual Destructor and a Child Class without a Virtual Destructor
Refactor_Advice: Declare the destructor as virtual in the child class to ensure proper cleanup of derived object resources.

Description: Initialization with Hard-Coded Network Resource Configuration Data
Refactor_Advice: Move hard-coded IP addresses or network identifiers to secure, environment-specific configuration files.

Description: Missing Serialization Control Element
Refactor_Advice: Implement explicit serialization version UIDs and correct serialization control methods for classes intended for transport/storage.

Description: Serializable Data Element Containing non-Serializable Item Elements
Refactor_Advice: Ensure all nested member objects within a serializable class also implement the serialization interface, or mark them as transient.

Description: Floating Point Comparison with Incorrect Operator
Refactor_Advice: Compare floating-point values using an acceptable tolerance threshold (epsilon) rather than direct strict equality operators (==).

Description: Parent Class without Virtual Destructor Method
Refactor_Advice: Declare the destructor in the base class as virtual to guarantee correct polymorphic destruction of derived instances.

Description: Class Instance Self Destruction Control Element
Refactor_Advice: Avoid 'delete this' idioms; instead, rely on standard object lifecycle management patterns or smart pointers to handle destruction safely.

Description: Data Access from Outside Designated Data Manager Component
Refactor_Advice: Route all database or file access operations strictly through the designated Data Access Object (DAO) or data manager layer.

Description: Class with Virtual Method without a Virtual Destructor
Refactor_Advice: Add a virtual destructor to any polymorphic base class to prevent resource leaks when instances are deleted.

Description: Synchronous Access of Remote Resource without Timeout
Refactor_Advice: Configure explicit and reasonable timeouts for all network calls to prevent the application thread from hanging indefinitely.

Description: Data Element containing Pointer Item without Proper Copy Control Element
Refactor_Advice: Implement a proper copy constructor and assignment operator (Rule of Three/Five) to manage deep copies of pointer members.

Description: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
Refactor_Advice: Validate all filesystem path inputs against a strict allowlist and resolve paths to their canonical absolute format before access.

Description: Relative Path Traversal
Refactor_Advice: Neutralize relative path sequences (like '../') by strictly validating inputs against allowed directory scopes.

Description: Absolute Path Traversal
Refactor_Advice: Reject any input containing absolute path definitions and construct file paths programmatically from a safe base directory.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command ('Command Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Avoid shelling out to the OS. Use safe, parameterized API bindings that bypass the shell interpreter entirely.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Do not pass user input directly into OS command execution functions; strictly sanitize inputs and use execution APIs that separate command from arguments.

Description: Argument Injection or Modification
Refactor_Advice: Strictly delimit and sanitize all arguments passed to downstream components or processes to prevent flag/argument injection.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
Refactor_Advice: Implement context-aware output encoding for all user-supplied data prior to rendering it in the DOM or HTML templates.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Enforce the exclusive use of parameterized queries or prepared statements for all database interactions.

Description: SQL Injection: Hibernate
Refactor_Advice: Avoid building dynamic HQL/SQL queries using string concatenation with user input; always use Hibernate's parameter binding methods.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query ('LDAP Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Apply strict escaping rules specific to LDAP syntax for any user-controlled input incorporated into LDAP search filters.

Description: XML Injection (aka Blind XPath Injection)
Refactor_Advice: Sanitize user inputs used in XML construction or XPath queries, and configure the XML parser to disallow arbitrary code execution.

Description: Improper Control of Resource Identifiers (‘Resource injection’)
Refactor_Advice: Implement strict validation and access control checks before allowing external input to dictate resource identifiers (e.g., port numbers, file IDs).

Description: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
Refactor_Advice: Enforce strict boundary checking before memory buffer access to prevent arbitrary read/write vulnerabilities.

Description: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
Refactor_Advice: Use safe bounded copy functions (e.g., strncpy instead of strcpy) to guarantee the input buffer does not overflow the output.

Description: Write-what-where condition
Refactor_Advice: Validate all pointer arithmetic and memory offsets to prevent attackers from overwriting arbitrary memory locations.

Description: Out-of-bounds Read
Refactor_Advice: Validate array indices and pointer offsets against buffer limits before executing read operations to prevent data leakage.

Description: Improper Handling of Length Parameter Inconsistency
Refactor_Advice: Validate that explicit length fields in parsed network payloads strictly match the actual data size before processing.

Description: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer
Refactor_Advice: Ensure pointer decrements and negative indices are boundary-checked to prevent reading or writing before the buffer's base address.

Description: Out-of-bounds Write
Refactor_Advice: Check bounds before writing to arrays, ensuring index variables do not exceed allocated limits to prevent memory corruption.

Description: Access of Memory Location After End of Buffer
Refactor_Advice: Prevent pointer increments from passing the buffer's tail end by enforcing strict upper-bound limits.

Description: Buffer Access with Incorrect Length Value
Refactor_Advice: Double-check length calculations passed to sequential read/write operations to ensure they do not exceed buffer bounds.

Description: Untrusted Pointer Dereference
Refactor_Advice: Never directly convert untrusted input into pointers; strictly validate any external data used to reference memory locations.

Description: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset
Refactor_Advice: Validate that offset calculations derived from untrusted sources fall safely within the target structured data boundaries.

Description: Access of Uninitialized Pointer
Refactor_Advice: Initialize all pointers to NULL or a valid memory address immediately upon declaration to prevent exploitation.

Description: Expired Pointer Dereference
Refactor_Advice: Set pointers to NULL immediately after freeing the memory they point to, preventing attackers from exploiting dangling pointers.

Description: Improper Validation of Array Index
Refactor_Advice: Validate array indices derived from untrusted input against strict upper and lower bounds before using them for data access.

Description: Use of Externally Controlled Format String
Refactor_Advice: Never pass unvalidated user input directly as the format string parameter to formatting functions (e.g., printf); use static strings.

Description: Unchecked Return Value
Refactor_Advice: Verify the return values of all critical security, I/O, and system API calls to handle potential failure states securely.

Description: Improper Resource Shutdown or Release
Refactor_Advice: Guarantee resource closure to prevent resource exhaustion attacks by utilizing try-with-resources or explicit 'finally' blocks.

Description: Improper Release of Memory Before Removing Last Reference ('Memory Leak')
Refactor_Advice: Ensure explicit deallocation of memory objects to prevent gradual memory consumption and subsequent Denial of Service.

Description: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Release network connections and memory immediately after use to prevent resource starvation.

Description: Missing Release of File Descriptor or Handle after Effective Lifetime
Refactor_Advice: Explicitly close file handles to prevent attackers from consuming all available descriptors and causing a DoS.

Description: Improper Protection of Alternate Path
Refactor_Advice: Implement unified authentication and authorization middleware to secure all potential access paths.

Description: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
Refactor_Advice: Verify file types using strict allowlists based on MIME types and magic numbers, and store them securely outside the web root.

Description: Use of Obsolete Function
Refactor_Advice: Replace deprecated or obsolete functions with their modernized, secure equivalents provided by updated standard libraries.

Description: Use of Incorrect Operator
Refactor_Advice: Review logical expressions to ensure correct operators are used to prevent security control bypasses.

Description: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
Refactor_Advice: Avoid deserializing complex objects from untrusted sources; utilize safe data formats like JSON or enforce strict class allowlists.

Description: Expression is Always False
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the logical condition to ensure security checks or access controls evaluate dynamically based on correct states.

Description: Expression Is Always True
Refactor_Advice: Refactor the logical condition to ensure security-critical branches (like auth failure handling) are actually reachable.

Description: Unchecked Input for Loop Condition
Refactor_Advice: Sanitize and strictly bound inputs used for loop conditions to prevent attackers from causing excessive CPU consumption.

Description: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference ('XXE')
Refactor_Advice: Explicitly disable DTD (Document Type Definition) and XML external entity processing in the XML parser configuration.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Data within XPath Expressions ('XPath Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Use precompiled or parameterized XPath queries to safely inject variables without altering the core XPath structure.

Description: CWE-652 Improper Neutralization of Data within XQuery Expressions ('XQuery Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Use parameterized queries or strictly validate input before embedding it into dynamic XQuery expressions.

Description: Improper Initialization
Refactor_Advice: Ensure complex resources are fully initialized and valid before their references are exposed, preventing access to unsecured states.

Description: Missing Initialization of a Variable
Refactor_Advice: Initialize all critical variables explicitly at the point of declaration to prevent attackers from reading residual memory.

Description: Use of uninitialized variable
Refactor_Advice: Assign a default, secure value to variables before any read operations occur.

Description: Improper Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Synchronize access to shared mutable state to prevent attackers from exploiting timing windows to corrupt state.

Description: Race Condition within a Thread
Refactor_Advice: Identify critical sections where shared resources are used concurrently and wrap them in mutual exclusion locks.

Description: Use of Singleton Pattern Without Synchronization in a Multithreaded Context
Refactor_Advice: Implement thread-safe initialization for Singletons to ensure critical shared objects cannot be maliciously duplicated.

Description: Unsynchronized Access to Shared Data in a Multithreaded Context
Refactor_Advice: Protect shared static variables across threads using appropriate synchronization locks to maintain data integrity.

Description: Improper Locking
Refactor_Advice: Ensure locks are acquired correctly and definitively released in a robust 'finally' block to prevent resource deadlocks.

Description: Missing Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Identify concurrent access points to shared resources and apply appropriate synchronization to prevent race conditions.

Description: Incorrect Synchronization
Refactor_Advice: Review synchronization blocks to ensure they lock on the correct shared object monitor.

Description: Operation on a Resource after Expiration or Release
Refactor_Advice: Invalidate resource references immediately upon release and verify authorization states before attempting subsequent operations.

Description: Double Free
Refactor_Advice: Nullify pointers immediately after freeing the associated memory to prevent heap corruption exploits.

Description: Use After Free
Refactor_Advice: Eliminate dangling pointers by clearing references after memory release to prevent arbitrary code execution vulnerabilities.

Description: Incorrect Conversion between Numeric Types
Refactor_Advice: Validate numeric ranges before type casting to prevent integer truncation that could bypass security checks.

Description: Unexpected Sign Extension
Refactor_Advice: Use unsigned data types when upcasting to prevent negative numbers from enabling boundary check bypasses.

Description: Signed to Unsigned Conversion Error
Refactor_Advice: Validate that a signed integer is positive before casting it to an unsigned type to prevent massive unintended allocations.

Description: Unsigned to Signed Conversion Error
Refactor_Advice: Ensure the unsigned value does not exceed the maximum capacity of the target signed type before casting.

Description: Numeric Truncation Error
Refactor_Advice: Check bounds before downcasting primitive types to prevent data loss that could bypass size constraints.

Description: Incorrect Calculation
Refactor_Advice: Audit arithmetic logic for critical decision-making code, adding comprehensive unit tests for mathematical correctness.

Description: Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size
Refactor_Advice: Double-check buffer allocation sizing logic to prevent attackers from causing overflows via undersized buffers.

Description: Divide By Zero
Refactor_Advice: Add conditional guards to explicitly verify that the denominator is strictly non-zero to prevent Denial of Service crashes.

Description: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
Refactor_Advice: Apply the Principle of Least Privilege by setting restrictive permissions (e.g., chmod) on sensitive files and directories.

Description: Insufficient Logging
Refactor_Advice: Log all security-critical events (authentication, authorization, state changes) with sufficient context to support incident forensics.

Description: Operator Precedence Logic Error
Refactor_Advice: Use explicit parentheses in complex expressions to force the intended order of operations, especially in security-critical logic.

Description: Uncontrolled Memory Allocation
Refactor_Advice: Enforce strict upper limits and validation checks on size parameters derived from external inputs before dynamic memory allocation.

Description: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
Refactor_Advice: Extract all hard-coded secrets from the source code and inject them dynamically via secure environment variables or a vault.

Description: Use of Hard-coded Password
Refactor_Advice: Do not commit passwords into version control. Abstract authentication secrets into external configuration files or secret managers.

Description: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key
Refactor_Advice: Manage cryptographic keys via secure key management systems (KMS) or HSMs rather than embedding them directly in source code.

Description: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop')
Refactor_Advice: Review loop control variables to ensure they are updated correctly so attackers cannot trigger infinite loops and DoS the service.

Description: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an Expression Language Statement ('Expression Language Injection')
Refactor_Advice: Disable dynamic EL resolution for user inputs, or rigorously sanitize data before processing it within Expression Language engines.

Description: Data Access Operations Outside of Expected Data Manager Component
Refactor_Advice: Route all data access operations strictly through the designated DAO layer to ensure security controls are uniformly applied.
